pathways.label: Pathway 1 — Free non-conviction expunction under Tenn. Code §§ 40-32-101(a) / 40-32-106
pathways.summary: Four pathways, considered by how the case ended. Non-conviction expunction, free (§ 40-32-101(a) / § 40-32-106) Available at NO cost where the case did not result in a conviction: Charge dismissed (for a reason other than successful diversion completion). Nolle prosequi (prosecutor declined to proceed).
ruleClauses: Four pathways, considered by how the case ended.
Non-conviction expunction, free (§ 40-32-101(a) / § 40-32-106)
Available at NO cost where the case did not result in a conviction:
Charge dismissed (for a reason other than successful diversion completion).
Nolle prosequi (prosecutor declined to proceed).
Grand jury returned a no true bill (no indictment).
Not guilty verdict, by judge or jury, on all counts.

Person was arrested and released without being charged. On a not-guilty verdict on all counts, the judge must ask the acquitted person whether they want the records destroyed; if yes, the court orders it immediately with no separate petition. For dismissals and nolle prosequi after the fact, the person files a petition (no fee).
Diversion expunction (§ 40-15-105 pretrial / § 40-35-313 judicial)
Two distinct diversion programs, both ending in expunction after successful completion — but a petition is still

Pretrial diversion suspends prosecution under a Memorandum of Understanding with the DA; the person does not plead or get found guilty, and on completion asks the court to send an Order of Expungement to the TBI (marked Suspension of Prosecution). Judicial diversion involves a conditional guilty plea with NO judgment of conviction entered; the court defers adjudication and places the person on probation (typically 6–18 months), and on successful completion discharges and dismisses the case, after which the person petitions to
A completed judicial diversion is not a conviction under Tennessee law.
Eligible-conviction expunction (§ 40-32-101(g) / § 40-32-107)
Since 2012 (and expanded repeatedly), Tennessee allows expunction of certain non-violent, non-DUI,
non-sexual convictions — most misdemeanors and a defined set of Class E felonies — after the person has
completed the entire sentence and the waiting period has run. If the eligibility requirements are met, there is a
rebuttable presumption that the petition should be granted. A $100 filing fee applies (now discretionary
Two-offense expunction (§ 40-32-101(k))
Since 2017, an “eligible petitioner” may expunge up to TWO offenses — either two misdemeanors, or one
felony and one misdemeanor — in a single lifetime use, provided each offense is independently eligible under

and each occurred before any conviction for an ineligible offense. The 2025 reorganization (§ 40-32-107) broadened this for offenses arising from the same incident or closely related charges. A person may use this two-offense option only once.
Tennessee Expunction — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma
Route by outcome. Non-conviction → free expunction (sometimes immediate at acquittal). Completed
diversion → petition to expunge (pretrial vs. judicial differ in mechanics). Eligible conviction → (g)/107
expunction after the wait; two eligible offenses → the once-in-a-lifetime (k) pathway.
Always tell users expunction is NOT automatic — a dismissal or completed diversion stays on
background checks until they file. This is the most common Tennessee misunderstanding.
Non-conviction expunction, free (§ 40-32-101(a) / § 40-32-106) Available at NO cost where the case did not result in a conviction:
waitingRules: Person was arrested and released without being charged. For dismissals and nolle prosequi after the fact, the person files a petition (no fee).

Pretrial diversion suspends prosecution under a Memorandum of Understanding with the DA; the person does not plead or get found guilty, and on completion asks the court to send an Order of Expungement to the TBI (marked Suspension of Prosecution). Judicial diversion involves a conditional guilty plea with NO judgment of conviction entered; the court defers adjudication and places the person on probation (typically 6–18 months), and on successful completion discharges and dismisses the case, after which the person petitions to
completed the entire sentence and the waiting period has run. If the eligibility requirements are met, there is a
fines, restitution, and court costs — then the waiting period runs from that completion. Non-conviction and
diversion expunctions have no level-based waiting period.

Misdemeanor conviction 5 years Sentence $100

Non-conviction and diversion expunctions have no level-based waiting period.

Route by outcome. Non-conviction → free expunction (sometimes immediate at acquittal). Completed
exclusionRules: The conviction pathway is limited to non-violent, non-DUI, non-sexual offenses. The following are
categorically NOT eligible for conviction expunction, regardless of time elapsed:
DUI convictions (T.C.A. § 55-10-401) and related serious traffic offenses.
Sexual offenses and any offense requiring sex-offender registration.
Class A and Class B felonies (the most serious felony classes).
Violent offenses and crimes against the person enumerated in the statute.
Domestic violence assault convictions and certain offenses against children.
Eligible Class E felonies (illustrative)
Among the Class E felonies that ARE eligible: theft (§ 39-14-103), forgery (§ 39-14-114), fraudulent use of a
offenses to the eligible list repeatedly (e.g., the 2021 expansion adding dozens of offenses), so the list grows
Any other conviction on the record (in-state, federal, or out-of-state and ineligible here) generally bars conviction expunction.
Prior expunction. The two-offense (k) pathway may be used only once in a lifetime.

Diversion eligibility differs. Judicial diversion is barred for Class A/B felonies, sexual offenses, DUI, vehicular assault, and where the person has a prior felony or a prior Class A misdemeanor with jail time, or any prior diversion.
The eligible Class E felony list is defined by enumerated Code sections and has been expanded
by the Legislature almost yearly; the 2025 reorganization also moved the conviction provisions to
§ 40-32-107. The agent must map the user's exact offense to its T.C.A. section and check it
against the current eligible/ineligible lists (the Nashville DA and TBI publish checklists) before
declaring eligibility — the offense label and class alone are not enough.

sourceSections.title: Eligibility rules
sourceSections.text:
Four pathways, considered by how the case ended.

Pathway 1 — Non-conviction expunction, free (§ 40-32-101(a) / § 40-32-106)
Available at NO cost where the case did not result in a conviction:

• Charge dismissed (for a reason other than successful diversion completion).
• Nolle prosequi (prosecutor declined to proceed).
• Grand jury returned a no true bill (no indictment).
• Not guilty verdict, by judge or jury, on all counts.
• Person was arrested and released without being charged.
On a not-guilty verdict on all counts, the judge must ask the acquitted person whether they want the records
destroyed; if yes, the court orders it immediately with no separate petition. For dismissals and nolle prosequi
after the fact, the person files a petition (no fee).

Pathway 2 — Diversion expunction (§ 40-15-105 pretrial / § 40-35-313 judicial)
Two distinct diversion programs, both ending in expunction after successful completion — but a petition is still
required. Pretrial diversion suspends prosecution under a Memorandum of Understanding with the DA; the
person does not plead or get found guilty, and on completion asks the court to send an Order of Expungement to
the TBI (marked Suspension of Prosecution). Judicial diversion involves a conditional guilty plea with NO
judgment of conviction entered; the court defers adjudication and places the person on probation (typically 6–18
months), and on successful completion discharges and dismisses the case, after which the person petitions to
expunge. A completed judicial diversion is not a conviction under Tennessee law.

Pathway 3 — Eligible-conviction expunction (§ 40-32-101(g) / § 40-32-107)
Since 2012 (and expanded repeatedly), Tennessee allows expunction of certain non-violent, non-DUI,
non-sexual convictions — most misdemeanors and a defined set of Class E felonies — after the person has
completed the entire sentence and the waiting period has run. If the eligibility requirements are met, there is a
rebuttable presumption that the petition should be granted. A $100 filing fee applies (now discretionary
with the clerk).

Pathway 4 — Two-offense expunction (§ 40-32-101(k))
Since 2017, an “eligible petitioner” may expunge up to TWO offenses — either two misdemeanors, or one
felony and one misdemeanor — in a single lifetime use, provided each offense is independently eligible under
(g) and each occurred before any conviction for an ineligible offense. The 2025 reorganization (§ 40-32-107)
broadened this for offenses arising from the same incident or closely related charges. A person may use this
two-offense option only once.

Tennessee Expunction — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

AGENT GUIDANCE
Route by outcome. Non-conviction → free expunction (sometimes immediate at acquittal). Completed
diversion → petition to expunge (pretrial vs. judicial differ in mechanics). Eligible conviction → (g)/107
expunction after the wait; two eligible offenses → the once-in-a-lifetime (k) pathway.
Always tell users expunction is NOT automatic — a dismissal or completed diversion stays on
background checks until they file. This is the most common Tennessee misunderstanding.

Tennessee Expunction — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

sourceSections.title: Waiting periods
sourceSections.text:
Conviction expunction requires completion of the ENTIRE sentence first — imprisonment, probation, parole,
fines, restitution, and court costs — then the waiting period runs from that completion. Non-conviction and
diversion expunctions have no level-based waiting period.

Pathway / matter Waiting period Runs from Fee

Non-conviction (dismissed, None — Free
nolle, no true bill, acquittal)

Pretrial / judicial diversion None (after Discharge & Clerk fee
completion) dismissal

Misdemeanor conviction 5 years Sentence $100
completion

Eligible Class E felony 5 years Sentence $100
completion

Eligible Class C or D felony 10 years Sentence $100
completion

Two-offense (k) — two misd. 5 years Most recent $100
or Class E + misd. completion

Two-offense (k) — includes a 10 years Most recent $100
Class C or D felony completion

Two threshold facts the agent must check. First, the conviction must have occurred on or after November 1,
1989 to be expungeable under the conviction pathway. Second, at the time of filing the person must have no
other conviction of any kind — including federal offenses and out-of-state offenses that would be ineligible in
Tennessee — except the offense(s) being expunged. Outstanding fines, restitution, or court costs mean the
sentence is not complete and the clock has not started.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Compute from completion of the ENTIRE sentence, including unpaid fines/restitution/costs — those stop
the clock from starting. Misdemeanor and Class E = 5 years; Class C/D = 10 years.
Confirm the person has no other convictions anywhere; a single ineligible conviction elsewhere generally
defeats conviction expunction. Verify the offense post-dates Nov 1, 1989.

Tennessee Expunction — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

sourceSections.title: Disqualifying / ineligible offenses
sourceSections.text:
The conviction pathway is limited to non-violent, non-DUI, non-sexual offenses. The following are
categorically NOT eligible for conviction expunction, regardless of time elapsed:

• DUI convictions (T.C.A. § 55-10-401) and related serious traffic offenses.
• Sexual offenses and any offense requiring sex-offender registration.
• Class A and Class B felonies (the most serious felony classes).
• Violent offenses and crimes against the person enumerated in the statute.
• Vehicular assault / vehicular homicide and similar.
• Domestic violence assault convictions and certain offenses against children.
• Offenses committed by a public official in an official capacity.

Eligible Class E felonies (illustrative)
Among the Class E felonies that ARE eligible: theft (§ 39-14-103), forgery (§ 39-14-114), fraudulent use of a
credit card (§ 39-14-118), vandalism, fraudulent/false insurance claims, and certain drug-possession offenses
(Schedule III/IV/V, or small amounts — under 0.5 grams — of Schedule II). The Legislature has added
offenses to the eligible list repeatedly (e.g., the 2021 expansion adding dozens of offenses), so the list grows
over time.

Other categorical limits
• Any other conviction on the record (in-state, federal, or out-of-state and ineligible here) generally bars
conviction expunction.
• Prior expunction. The two-offense (k) pathway may be used only once in a lifetime.
• Diversion eligibility differs. Judicial diversion is barred for Class A/B felonies, sexual offenses, DUI,
vehicular assault, and where the person has a prior felony or a prior Class A misdemeanor with jail time, or
any prior diversion.

VERIFY BEFORE RELYING
The eligible Class E felony list is defined by enumerated Code sections and has been expanded
by the Legislature almost yearly; the 2025 reorganization also moved the conviction provisions to
§ 40-32-107. The agent must map the user's exact offense to its T.C.A. section and check it
against the current eligible/ineligible lists (the Nashville DA and TBI publish checklists) before
declaring eligibility — the offense label and class alone are not enough.

AGENT GUIDANCE
DUI and sexual offenses are the two hard, permanent bars users most often misjudge — neither is ever
expungeable. Class A/B felonies are out; only specific Class E (and the lower misdemeanors) qualify.
Because the eligible list keeps expanding, check the current Nashville DA / TBI checklist rather than
assuming a borderline Class E felony is in or out.

Tennessee Expunction — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

sourceSections.title: Case outcome rules
sourceSections.text:
Outcome drives the pathway, the cost, and whether a petition (vs. an immediate order) is needed. This table is
the agent's primary routing logic for Tennessee.

Case outcome Pathway Route Cost / wait

Not guilty on all counts § 40-32-101(a) Judge asks; immediate order Free / none

Dismissed / nolle / no § 40-32-101(a) Petition (free) Free / none
true bill

Arrested, never charged § 40-32-101(a) Petition (free) Free / none

Pretrial diversion completed § 40-15-105 Petition; order to TBI Clerk fee / none

Judicial diversion completed § 40-35-313 Petition after dismissal Clerk fee / none

Misdemeanor / Class E § (g) / 107 Petition $100 / 5 yrs
felony conviction

Class C or D felony § (g) / 107 Petition $100 / 10 yrs
(eligible)

Two eligible offenses § (k) Petition (once in lifetime) $100 / 5–10 yrs

DUI / sexual / Class A-B — Not eligible —
/ violent

Effect of relief: a Tennessee expunction removes and destroys the public records of the case, and the person is
restored in the eyes of the law to the status they held before the arrest — they may lawfully deny the arrest and
charge in most contexts. Note the limits: federal systems and security-clearance background checks may still
see an underlying guilty plea (e.g., from a diversion), and the relief reaches Tennessee records only. If the
petition is denied, the person may reapply after two years.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Tennessee expunction is real erasure of the state record — stronger than the 'sealing' of many states —
but it does not reach federal records or undo a guilty plea visible to federal clearance checks. Set that
expectation for diversion cases especially.
If a petition is denied, advise the user they can reapply after two years.

Tennessee Expunction — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

sourceSections.title: Required forms
sourceSections.text:
Tennessee expunction is filed in the court where the case originated. The TBI and the Administrative Office of
the Courts publish forms; the TBI also runs a request-for-certification step. A petition is required for every
pathway except the immediate at-acquittal order.

Core components of an expunction filing
1 Petition / Order of Expungement — names the petitioner, date of birth, social security number, the offense
to be expunged, the state control number (per § 8-4-115), the date and cause of the dismissal or the conviction
details, and the statutory basis. A TBI PDF-fillable form is available.
2 TBI request-for-certification (§ 40-32-102) — for conviction and diversion expunctions, the court
completes and submits a certification form to the TBI before entering the order; the TBI provides the
certificate to the clerk. (Not required for some non-conviction expunctions under §§ 40-32-106 / 109 unless
the case came through diversion.)

3 Diversion marking — pretrial diversion orders are marked “Suspension of Prosecution § 40-15-105”;
judicial diversion orders reference § 40-35-313.

4 Proof of sentence completion — for conviction expunctions, documentation that all terms (including fines,
restitution, and costs) are satisfied and the waiting period has run.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Use the TBI PDF-fillable expungement order where available; electronic signatures are accepted via the
TBI FlexCheck process. For conviction/diversion cases, remember the court's § 40-32-102 certification
step precedes entry of the order.
Capture the state control number and the exact disposition early — the order cannot be completed
without them.

Tennessee Expunction — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

sourceSections.title: Filing instructions
sourceSections.text:
1 Get the record. Obtain a TBI criminal history and the court disposition to confirm the outcome, the statutory
basis, sentence completion, and the state control number.

2 Confirm the pathway and eligibility. Non-conviction vs. diversion vs. conviction; the eligible-offense lists;
the waiting period from full sentence completion; the no-other-conviction requirement; and the Nov 1, 1989
date for conviction expunction.

3 Prepare the petition / order of expungement (TBI fillable form) with the petitioner's identifiers, the
offense, the disposition, and the statutory citation (confirm 40-32-101 vs. the reorganized 40-32-106/107 with
the clerk).
4 File in the court where the case originated — the county criminal/circuit/general sessions court clerk —
and pay the fee ($100 for conviction expunction, discretionary with the clerk and payable in installments; free
for non-conviction).

5 DA review. The District Attorney may challenge the petition; for eligible convictions there is a rebuttable
presumption in favor of granting. The judge may hold a hearing if contested.

6 Court certification to TBI. For conviction/diversion cases, the court submits the § 40-32-102 certification to
the TBI and, once granted, must forward the expunction order to the TBI within thirty (30) days.

7 Destruction of records. On the granted order, the clerk and the TBI remove and destroy the public records of
the case. Keep a certified copy of the order.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Venue is the originating court's county — a person with cases in several counties files separately in each.
Confirm the right clerk (criminal, circuit, or general sessions) for the case.
Lean on the rebuttable presumption for eligible convictions: a complete, well-documented petition with
proof of sentence completion is presumptively granted.

Tennessee Expunction — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

sourceSections.title: Court-specific quirks
sourceSections.text:
• The 2025 reorganization. § 40-32-101 was split into §§ 40-32-106 (non-conviction), 40-32-107
(conviction), and 40-32-108; the substance carried forward but citations changed — confirm with the clerk or
DA which the county uses.
• Not automatic. Unlike Clean Slate states, Tennessee has no automatic sealing; every clearance requires a
filed petition (except the immediate order at a not-guilty verdict).
• Immediate order at acquittal. On a not-guilty verdict on all counts, the judge must ask the person if they
want records destroyed and can order it on the spot — no petition.
• Rebuttable presumption to grant. For eligible convictions, § (g)(5)(B) creates a presumption the petition
should be granted, shifting the practical burden to the DA to object.
• One-time two-offense use. The (k) pathway is a once-in-a-lifetime option; using it forecloses a later second
use.
• Fee history. The old $180 expunction fee was eliminated in 2019; the current conviction fee is $100, made
discretionary with the clerk in 2021 and payable in installments — non-conviction expunction is always free.
• Diversion is not erasure by itself. Completing pretrial or judicial diversion dismisses the case but leaves it
visible until a separate expunction petition is filed.
• TBI 30-day rule. The court must forward a granted expunction order to the TBI within 30 days.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Confirm the statutory citation with the county before drafting — the 2025 renumbering means forms may
show 40-32-101 or 40-32-106/107.
For diversion users, stress the separate expunction petition: completing diversion is not the same as
clearing the record.

Tennessee Expunction — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

sourceSections.title: Fee information
sourceSections.text:
Item Amount Note

Non-conviction expunction $0 Statute bars any fee (dismissed, nolle, no true bill, acquittal)

Conviction expunction (§ g / 107) $100 Discretionary with clerk since 2021; installments allowed

Two-offense expunction (§ k) $100 Single filing for up to two offenses

Diversion expunction Clerk fee Pretrial / judicial diversion completion

TBI criminal history (background check) TBI fee To confirm record before filing

Attorney fees (if represented) Varies Not required; legal-aid and DA expungement clinics exist

The cost picture is favorable. Non-conviction expunction is free by statute. The conviction-expunction fee
dropped from $180 to $100 in 2019, became discretionary with the court clerk in 2021, and may be paid in
installments — so a person who cannot pay up front can still proceed. The realistic out-of-pocket cost beyond
the filing fee is the TBI background-check fee used to confirm the record. Expungement clinics run by some
District Attorneys (e.g., Nashville) assist for free.

AGENT GUIDANCE
Lead with the free non-conviction route and the installment option for the $100 conviction fee; cost is
rarely a hard barrier in Tennessee. Point users to DA expungement clinics where available.
Budget the TBI background-check fee as the baseline expense for confirming the record before filing.

Tennessee Expunction — Agent Training Reference · LegalEase / Wilma

sourceSections.title: Criminal history (TBI) request process
sourceSections.text:
Reviewing the record first confirms the disposition, the state control number, and sentence completion, and
reveals any other conviction that would bar conviction expunction.

sourceSections.title: Sample petitions and completed examples
sourceSections.text:
Tennessee expunction is filed on a petition/order in the originating court. The examples below show structure
and key recitals for the main pathways. Names, dates, and identifiers are fictional and illustrative.

Example A — Petition / Order of Expunction, eligible conviction (§ 40-32-101(g) / 107)
IN THE CRIMINAL COURT FOR DAVIDSON COUNTY, TENNESSEE
STATE OF TENNESSEE v. ANDRE L. CARTER Case No. 2018-A-1234

PETITION FOR EXPUNCTION (T.C.A. 40-32-101(g) / 40-32-107)
1. Petitioner: Andre L. Carter; DOB 07/22/1992; SSN XXX-XX-4471;
State Control No. TN01234567.
2. Offense: Theft of property (Class E felony), T.C.A. 39-14-103,
convicted 03/14/2019; the offense occurred after 11/01/1989.
3. Petitioner completed the entire sentence -- probation, fines,
restitution, and court costs -- on 03/14/2020.
4. More than five (5) years have elapsed since completion.

sourceSections.title: NON-CONVICTION EXPUNCTION (40-32-101(a) / 40-32-106) — FREE:
sourceSections.text:
A charge dismissed / nolle prossed / no-true-billed, or an
acquittal, is expunged at no cost. On a not-guilty verdict on
all counts, the judge asks the person and may order destruction
IMMEDIATELY -- no petition. For a later dismissal, file the
free petition.

sourceSections.title: JUDICIAL DIVERSION (40-35-313):
sourceSections.text:
Conditional guilty plea, no judgment of conviction entered;
on completing probation the case is dismissed. The person then
PETITIONS to expunge (a clerk fee applies). Completed judicial
diversion is not a conviction under Tennessee law.
